 the safety problem. if you transfer money between two bank accounts — debit account a, then credit account b — and the system crashes between those two steps, the money vanishes. transactions group operations so they either all succeed or all roll back: begin; update accounts set balance = balance - 100 where id = 1; update accounts set balance = balance + 100 where id = 2; commit; — if anything fails, neither account changes. true-false a database is just a fancy name for a spreadsheet. databases are purpose-built software that can handle millions of records, enforce data rules, and support many users at once — far beyond what spreadsheets can do. in a relational database, data is organized into tables with rows and columns. tables (with columns for categories and rows for records) are the fundamental structure of relational databases. sqltablediagram sql basics: select, from, where sql (structured query language, often pronounced "sequel") is the language you use to talk to a relational database. the most common operation is reading data, and the basic pattern is:

 select column1, column2 from tablename where condition;

 for example, select name, marks from students where class = 10; asks: "give me the name and marks of every student in class 10." select means "all columns." if you leave out the where clause, you get every row in the table.

 sql is declarative — you describe what data you want, not how to get it. compare finding students with marks above 80:

 python (imperative — you write every step):
results = []
for student in students:
 if student["marks"] > 80:
 results.append(student["name"])

 sql (declarative — you describe the goal):
select name from students where marks > 80;

 the database engine figures out the fastest way to find those rows. sql was designed in the 1970s and is still the standard today. almost every app you use — instagram, google, zomato, banking apps — runs sql queries behind the scenes every time you tap a button. sqlqueryflowdiagram filtering and sorting: order by, limit, like, and/or real-world queries almost always need more than a simple where. order by sorts your results: select name, marks from students order by marks desc; returns students ranked from highest to lowest marks. add limit 5 to get only the top five. these two clauses together are how leaderboards and "top 10" lists work in every app.

 like lets you search for patterns in text. select name from students where name like 'a%'; finds all students whose name starts with "a". the % symbol means "anything can follow." you can combine conditions with and and or: where class = 10 and marks > 80 finds high-scoring students in class 10, while where class = 10 or class = 12 finds students in either class. these operators let you express surprisingly complex questions in a single readable sentence — no loops, no if-statements, just a clear description of the data you want. match each sql clause to its purpose sort results by a column restrict the number of rows returned search for text patterns using wildcards require multiple conditions to be true filter rows that match a condition sqlfiltersortdiagram joins: combining tables the real power of relational databases comes from joins — combining data from two or more tables in a single query. suppose you have a "students" table (rollnumber, name, class) and a "marks" table (rollnumber, subject, score). to see each student's name alongside their scores, you write:

 select students.name, marks.subject, ma

title: Ecology & Population Science
tagline: How populations grow, ecosystems connect, and species survive — or don\
searchText:
