½gt² r = v₀² sin 2θ / g h = v₀² sin²θ / (2g) 26.8 m 154 m t = 2v₀ sin θ / g 5.6 seconds air resistance f_d = ½ρv²c_da m(dv/dt) = mg − ½ρv²c_da magnus effect perfectly inelastic collision f = δp/δt 420 n 14,000 m/s² elastic collisions v₁\' = (m₁−m₂)v₁/(m₁+m₂) v₂\' = 2m₁v₁/(m₁+m₂) head injury criterion (hic) rigid hammer 2d collisions forces-and-motion forces & motion newton\'s laws, gravity, friction, and why woodpeckers don\'t get headaches. woodpeckers-drum the-little-boat kite-festival flying-squirrel lotus-float ferrymans-riddle newton\'s first law — inertia an object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion keeps moving in a straight line at constant speed, unless a force acts on it. this is the law of inertia. it explains why you lurch forward when a bus brakes suddenly — your body was moving with the bus, and when the bus stops, your body wants to keep going. inertia also depends on mass: a heavy object resists changes in motion more than a light one. a loaded truck needs much more braking force than a bicycle at the same speed. in everyday life, friction and air resistance are always acting, which is why things eventually slow down — but in the vacuum of space, a spacecraft launched at a certain speed will coast at that speed forever unless something pushes or pulls it. newton\'s first law is really a special case of the second law when net force is zero: if σf = 0, then a = 0, meaning velocity stays constant. to calculate whether an object accelerates, you sum all forces as vectors. a 1,000 kg car braking with 5,000 n of friction and driven by 5,000 n of engine force has σf = 0, so it cruises at constant speed. if the driver releases the accelerator, σf = −5,000 n, giving a = f/m = −5,000/1,000 = −5 m/s² — the car decelerates at 5 m/s². inertia is quantified by mass: the si unit of mass (kg) is literally defined by how much a body resists acceleration. a 20,000 kg truck at 30 m/s has the same speed as a 1 kg cricket ball at 30 m/s, but stopping the truck requires 20,000× more force over the same time. in non-inertial reference frames (accelerating frames), newton\'s first law appears to break down — objects seem to accelerate without any real force. to fix the math, we introduce pseudo-forces (fictitious forces). in a rotating frame, the coriolis force (f = −2m(ω × v)) deflects moving objects sideways, and the centrifugal force (f = −mω × (ω × r)) pushes them outward. these are not real forces — no physical interaction produces them — but they are essential for calculations inside rotating systems like earth. in special relativity, inertia increases with speed: the relativistic momentum is p = γmv, where γ = 1/√(1 − v²/c²). at 90% of light speed, γ ≈ 2.29, so a particle behaves as if it has 2.29× its rest mass. this is why particle accelerators need exponentially more energy to push particles closer to light speed — the effective inertia grows without bound as v → c. newton\'s second law — force, mass, acceleration the second law is often written as f = m x a: force equals mass times acceleration. this means a larger force produces a greater acceleration, and a heavier object needs more force to accelerate at the same rate. when you kick a football, the force of your foot determines how quickly the ball speeds up. kick it harder (more force), and it accelerates faster and travels further. try kicking a bowling ball with the same force, and it barely moves because its mass is much greater. this law is the foundation of nearly all engineering calculations — from designing car engines to planning rocket launches. every time you push, pull, or throw something, you are living newton\'s second law. the full vector form is f = ma, or more precisely σf = ma (the net force). example: a 0.45 kg football is kicked with 900 n of force for 0.01 seconds. acceleration = f/m = 900/0.45 = 2,000 m/s². using v = u + at (starting from rest, u = 0): v = 0 + 2,000 × 0.01 = 20 m/s (about 72 km/h). for a 6 kg bowling ball with the same 900 n kick: a = 900/6 = 150 m/s², and v = 150 × 0.01 = 1.5 m/s — barely a gentle roll. the impulse form is also useful: f × δt = m × δv (force × time = change in momentum). this is why airbags save lives — they increase the collision time δt, reducing the peak force f for the same momentum change. newton\'s second law in its most general form is f = dp/dt (force equals the rate of change of momentum), which handles cases where mass changes — like a rocket burning fuel. for a rocket: f = vexhaust × (dm/dt), the famous tsiolkovsky rocket equation. in lagrangian mechanics (university physics), f = ma is replaced by the euler-lagrange equation: d/dt(∂l/∂q̇) − ∂l/∂q = 0, where l = t − v (kinetic minus potential energy). this formulation handles constrained systems (pendulums, rolling wheels) far more elegantly than vector forces. in quantum mechanics, f = ma has no direct analogue — instead, the time evolution of a particle\'s state follows the schrödinger equation: iℏ ∂ψ/∂t = ĥψ. the expectation values of position and momentum still obey newton\'s law on average (ehrenfest\'s theorem), bridging classical and quantum physics. newton\'s third law — action and reaction newtonforcediagram for every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. when you jump, your legs push down on the ground, and the ground pushes you up with exactly the same force. a rocket engine works the same way: it pushes hot gas downward out of the nozzle, and the gas pushes the rocket upward. when a woodpecker hammers a tree trunk at up to 20 strikes per second, the tree pushes back against the bird\'s beak with equal force. woodpeckers survive this because their skulls have built-in shock absorbers — spongy bone, a hyoid bone that wraps around the skull like a seatbelt, and a beak that is slightly flexible. engineers have studied woodpecker skulls to design better protective helmets. the third law means forces always come in action-reaction pairs that act on different objects. when you stand on the floor: you push the floor down with your weight w = mg (say 60 × 9.8 = 588 n), and the floor pushes you up with a normal force n = 588 n. these are equal and opposite, but they act on different bodies (you and the floor), so they do not cancel out. a common mistake: thinking action-reaction pairs cancel. they never do, because they act on different objects. for a rocket: exhaust gas mass flow rate ṁ = 500 kg/s at exhaust vel

title: Genetics & DNA
tagline: The code of life — how DNA determines whether a seed becomes bamboo or a banyan.
searchText:
genetics & dna the code of life — how dna determines whether a seed becomes bamboo or a banyan. dna structure genes and chromosomes mendelian inheritance punnett squares mutations and variation 2 nm wide 10 base pairs per full turn 3.4 nm 3.2 billion base pairs nucleosome b-dna a-dna z-dna topoisomerases central dogma of molecular biology dna → rna → protein telomeres hayflick limit karyotype telomerase alt hi-c topologically associating domains enhancer hijacking law of segregation law of independent assortment dihybrid cross 9:3:3:1 incomplete dominance codominance sex-linked inheritance polygenic heritability gwas epistasis quantitative trait loci (qtl) mapping marker-assisted selection dihybrid punnett square chi-squared (χ²) test χ² = σ(o − e)² / e 0.47 bayesian probability likelihood ratios 1/100 linkage analysis silent missense nonsense codon 6 of β-globin changes from gag (glu) → gug (val) heterozygote advantage cpg dinucleotides crispr-cas9 base editors genetics-and-dna genetics & dna the code of life — how dna determines whether a seed becomes bamboo or a banyan. coconut-jackfruit orchid-colors takin-face dna structure deoxyribonucleic acid — dna — is the molecule that stores the instructions for building and operating every living organism. its structure, famously described by james watson and francis crick in 1953 (with critical x-ray crystallography data from rosalind franklin and maurice wilkins), is a double helix: two long strands wound around each other like a twisted ladder. the "rails" of the ladder are made of alternating sugar (deoxyribose) and phosphate groups — the sugar-phosphate backbone. the "rungs" are pairs of nitrogen-containing bases: adenine (a) always pairs with thymine (t), and guanine (g) always pairs with cytosine (c). these base-pairing rules mean the two strands are complementary — if you know the sequence of one strand, you can deduce the other.

the double helix is held together by hydrogen bonds between the base pairs — two bonds between a and t, three between g and c. these bonds are individually weak but collectively strong enough to keep the helix stable under normal conditions, yet weak enough to be "unzipped" by enzymes when the cell needs to read or copy the dna. a single human cell contains about 2 metres of dna packed into a nucleus just 6 micrometres across — an extraordinary feat of folding. the dna wraps around protein spools called histones, and these bead-like units (nucleosomes) coil and loop into increasingly compact structures.

the sequence of bases along a dna strand encodes information in a way that is conceptually similar to how the sequence of letters in a sentence encodes meaning. a gene is a specific segment of dna whose base sequence provides the instructions for making a particular protein (or sometimes a functional rna molecule). the human genome contains roughly 20,000–25,000 protein-coding genes, but these make up only about 1.5% of our total dna. the remaining 98.5% — once dismissively called "junk dna" — includes regulatory sequences that control when and where genes are switched on, structural dna, and sequences whose functions are still being discovered. the double helix has specific dimensions: it is 2 nm wide, with 10 base pairs per full turn and a pitch (rise per turn) of 3.4 nm — so each base pair is spaced 0.34 nm apart. the two strands run antiparallel: one runs 5' → 3' and the other 3' → 5' (the numbers refer to carbon atoms on the deoxyribose sugar). the human genome contains 3.2 billion base pairs on 23 chromosome pairs. if all the dna in one cell were stretched out, it would be 2 m long; all the dna in your body (37 trillion cells) would stretch to the sun and back 600 times. dna is packaged by wrapping 147 bp around a histone octamer (2 each of h2a, h2b, h3, h4) to form a nucleosome. nucleosomes are connected by 50 bp of linker dna, giving a "beads on a string" structure that further coils into a 30 nm fibre, then into loops attached to a protein scaffold. dna exists in three major structural forms: b-dna (the watson-crick form — right-handed helix, 10 bp/turn, most common in cells), a-dna (right-handed, 11 bp/turn, wider and shorter, found in dehydrated conditions and in dna-rna hybrids), and z-dna (left-handed helix, 12 bp/turn, found at sequences with alternating purine-pyrimidine repeats like cgcgcg). z-dna may form transiently during transcription and has been implicated in gene regulation. topoisomerases manage dna supercoiling: type i cuts one strand, allowing rotation to relieve torsional stress; type ii (e.g., dna gyrase in bacteria) cuts both strands and passes another duplex through the gap, introducing negative supercoils essential for compaction. many antibiotics (fluoroquinolones) target bacterial gyrase. the encode project (2012) found that 80% of the genome is biochemically active (transcribed, bound by proteins, or carrying chromatin marks), challenging the "junk dna" label — though the functional significance of much of this activity remains debated. dnadoublehelixdiagram genes and chromosomes a gene is a discrete segment of dna that codes for a specific protein or functional rna molecule. genes range in length from a few hundred to over two million base pairs. each gene's base sequence is read in groups of three (called codons), with each codon specifying one of the 20 amino acids that are the building blocks of proteins. the process of reading a gene involves two steps: transcription (copying the dna sequence into a messenger rna molecule in the nucleus) and translation (reading the mrna at a ribosome to assemble a chain of amino acids that folds into a functional protein). proteins then carry out nearly every function in the cell — they are enzymes, structural components, signalling molecules, and more.

chromosomes are the physical structures that package dna for cell division. each chromosome is a single, enormously long dna molecule wrapped around histone proteins and coiled into a compact form. humans have 46 chromosomes arranged in 23 pairs — 22 pairs of autosomes (non-sex chromosomes) and one pair of sex chromosomes (xx in females, xy in males). you inherit one chromosome of each pair from your mother (via the egg) and one from your father (via the sperm). different species have different chromosome numbers: dogs have 78, rice has 24, and the atlas blue butterfly has about 450. chromosome number has no relationship to organism complexity — what matters is the information encoded in the dna sequences those chromosomes carry. the central dogma of molecular biology (crick, 1958) describes information flow: dna → rna → protein. transcription copies a gene's dna sequence into mrna in the nucleus; translation reads mrna at ribosomes to build proteins. each codon (3-base sequence) maps to one of 20 amino acids via the genetic code: aug = methionine (start), uaa/uag/uga = stop. human chromosome 1 is the largest (249 million bp, 2,000 genes); chromosome 21 is the smallest autosome (48 million bp, 200 genes). telomeres — repetitive ttaggg sequences at chromosome ends (5,000–15,000 bp) — shorten by 50–200 bp per cell division, acting as a mitotic clock that limits cells to 50–70 divisions (the hayflick limit). the karyotype is used clinically to detect abnormalities: trisomy 21 (down syndrome), monosomy x (turner syndrome, 45,x). telomere biology connects chromosome structure to ageing and cancer. telomerase (a reverse transcriptase with rna template hterc) extends telomeres in stem and germ cells. most somatic cells lack telomerase, so telomeres progressively shorten, triggering replicative senescence via p53/rb when they reach 4 kb. 85% of cancers reactivate telomerase; the remainder use the alt pathway (homologous recombination-based). blackburn, greider, and szostak won the 2009 nobel prize for telomerase discovery. hi-c (chromosome conformation capture) reveals that chromosomes are organized into topologically associating domains (tad

title: Geology & Earth Science
tagline: The ground under your feet is moving, mountains are growing, and rocks tell stories.
searchText:
geology & earth science the ground under your feet is moving, mountains are growing, and rocks tell stories. the earth is an egg tectonic plates — the ground is moving under your feet mountains are still growing volcanoes — pressure cookers underground earthquakes — when plates get stuck then snap rocks tell stories — three types of rock match each rock type to its formation process fossils — nature\ erosion — water is patient and powerful freeze-thaw weathering the shell = the crust. the egg white = the mantle. convection the yolk = the core. check yourself: seismology mohorovičić discontinuity (moho) gutenberg discontinuity inge lehmann d″ layer magnetic field paleomagnetism 15 major tectonic plates pangaea convection currents divergent boundary convergent boundary transform boundary try this: 1 centimetre per year erosion tectonic uplift isostatic adjustment did you know? analogy: magma lava viscosity low viscosity high viscosity seismic waves focus epicenter p-waves (primary) s-waves (secondary) richter scale 32 times igneous rocks granite basalt obsidian sedimentary rocks sandstone limestone shale metamorphic rocks marble slate quartzite rock cycle sedimentary rock replace petrification trace fossils coprolites 3.5 billion years old water ice frost wedging wind gravity biological erosion expands when it freezes freeze-thaw weathering 207 mpa pried apart freezing point tensile strength frequency water availability rock porosity crack density frozen fringe saturation coefficient geology-and-earth geology & earth science the ground under your feet is moving, mountains are growing, and rocks tell stories. the earth is an egg earthlayersdiagram if you could slice the earth in half like a boiled egg, you would see layers.

the shell = the crust. this is the thin, solid outer layer where we live. under the continents, it is 30-70 km thick (continental crust). under the oceans, it is only 5-10 km thick (oceanic crust). compared to the whole earth, the crust is thinner than an eggshell is to an egg.

the egg white = the mantle. this is a 2,900 km thick layer of hot, dense rock. it is not liquid, but it is not quite solid either — over millions of years, it flows like extremely thick honey. this slow churning (called convection) is what drives the movement of tectonic plates on the surface. the upper mantle reaches temperatures of about 1,000 degrees celsius; the lower mantle exceeds 3,000 degrees celsius.

the yolk = the core. the outer core (2,200 km thick) is liquid iron and nickel at 4,000-5,000 degrees celsius. it is this swirling liquid metal that generates earth's magnetic field — the invisible shield that deflects solar radiation and makes compasses work. the inner core (1,200 km radius) is solid iron at over 5,000 degrees celsius — as hot as the surface of the sun. it is solid despite the extreme temperature because the pressure at the center of the earth is over 3.6 million times atmospheric pressure, which forces the atoms to pack tightly into a solid.

check yourself: the inner core is as hot as the surface of the sun. why is it solid? (answer: the immense pressure — 3.6 million atmospheres — forces atoms together so tightly that they cannot flow as a liquid.) earth’s internal structure is probed by seismology. p-waves travel through both solids and liquids; s-waves only through solids. a seismometer on the far side of the earth from an earthquake detects p-waves but not s-waves in a "shadow zone" between 104° and 140° angular distance, proving the outer core is liquid. deeper analysis reveals velocity discontinuities: the mohorovičić discontinuity (moho) at 5–70 km depth marks the crust–mantle boundary (p-wave velocity jumps from 6.5 to 8 km/s); the gutenberg discontinuity at 2,900 km marks the mantle–core boundary (p-wave velocity drops from 13.7 to 8 km/s as the medium changes from solid to liquid). the inner core was discovered by inge lehmann in 1936 from faint p-wave reflections within the shadow zone. modern seismic tomography constructs 3d maps of earth’s interior velocity structure from thousands of earthquake recordings, revealing mantle plumes (rising columns of hot material) and subducted slabs (cold, dense remnants of oceanic crust sinking to the core–mantle boundary). the d″ layer at the base of the mantle shows ultra-low velocity zones (ulvzs) interpreted as partial melt or iron-enriched silicates. earth’s magnetic field is generated by the geodynamo — convection of liquid iron in the outer core, modelled by solving the magnetohydrodynamic (mhd) equations. the field reverses polarity at irregular intervals (averaging 300,000 years); the last reversal (brunhes–matuyama) was 780,000 years ago. paleomagnetism — the record of ancient field orientation locked into cooling lava — provided some of the strongest evidence for continental drift and sea-floor spreading. tectonic plates — the ground is moving under your feet plateboundarydiagram the earth's crust is not a single unbroken shell. it is cracked into about 15 major tectonic plates that float on the semi-liquid mantle below, like crackers floating on soup. these plates are in constant motion — typically 2-10 centimetres per year, roughly the speed your fingernails grow.

that sounds slow, but over millions of years it reshapes the world. 200 million years ago, all the continents were joined in a single supercontinent called pangaea. tectonic movement broke it apart and pushed the pieces to where they are today. south america and africa were once joined — which is why their coastlines fit together like puzzle pieces and why you find identical fossils on both continents.

plates move because of convection currents in the mantle. hot rock rises from near the core, flows sideways under the crust, cools, and sinks back down. this circulation drags the plates along.

where plates meet, three things can happen:
- divergent boundary — plates pull apart, magma rises to fill the gap (mid-ocean ridges, like the mid-atlantic ridge)
- convergent boundary — plates push together, one slides under the other (subduction), creating mountains and deep ocean trenches
- transform boundary — plates slide past each other sideways (like the san andreas fault in california)

try this: take two sheets of paper and lay them flat on a table touching edge to edge. now slowly push them together. watch how they buckle upward — that is exactly how mountains form at convergent boundaries.

check yourself: if india is moving north at about 5 cm per year, how far will it have traveled in 1 million years? (answer: 5 cm x 1,000,000 = 5,000,000 cm = 50 km — enough to significantly change the geography.) mountains are still growing geomountaingrowthdiagram the himalayas — the tallest mountain range on earth — are still growing. mount everest gains about 1 centimetre per year because the indian tectonic plate is still crashing into the eurasian plate at about 5 cm per year.

this collision started about 50 million years ago when the indian subcontinent, which had been drifting north after breaking away from africa, slammed into asia. the force was (and is) enormous — enough to crumple the earth's crust upward like a car bonnet in a head-on collision.

but mountains do not grow forever. erosion fights back. wind, water, ice, and gravity constantly wear down mountains. the appalachian mountains in eastern north america were once as tall as the himalayas — about 450 million years ago. after hundreds of millions of years of erosion with no new tectonic uplift, they have been worn down to gentle, rounded hills.

the height of a mountain at any moment is the balance between:
- tectonic uplift (pushing up) — active at convergent plate boundaries
- erosion (wearing down) — happening everywhere, all the time
- isostatic adjustment — the crust floats on the mantle like an iceberg in water. as erosion removes material from the top, the mountain rises slightly, like a ship rising as you unload c

title: Geometry Essentials
tagline: Shapes, angles, and spatial reasoning — from bamboo huts to bridge arches.
searchText:
geometry essentials shapes, angles, and spatial reasoning — from bamboo huts to bridge arches. types of angles triangles and the pythagorean theorem 3d shapes: volume and surface area try it — areas & volumes transformations what is an angle? vertex angle types by size: how to measure: key angle relationships: complementary supplementary vertical angles angle relationships with parallel lines: worked example: one known angle non-euclidean geometry changes the rules: spherical (elliptic) hyperbolic (saddle) gauss-bonnet theorem the simplest polygon: 180° classification by sides: classification by angles: the pythagorean theorem example: ladder against a wall 4 metres the converse: 3-4-5 triple pythagorean triples generating primitive triples: worked problem: 12 metres generalizations of the pythagorean theorem: law of cosines: n-dimensional distance: fermat's last theorem what is a circle? radius key measurements: circle vocabulary: chord tangent arc sector sector area: arc length and sector area (radians): 11.78 cm 88.36 cm² key theorems: inscribed angle theorem thales' theorem circle equation: apollonius circles isoperimetric inequality why 3d matters: key formulas: worked example: cylindrical water drum composite shapes: example: total 282.7 m³ 75 pi = 235.6 m² cavalieri's principle: curse of dimensionality 2 + b what is a transformation? rigid transformations symmetry line symmetry rotational symmetry japi hat coordinate descriptions: (−4, 3) similar symmetry groups: group 17 distinct symmetry groups 230 space groups geometry-essentials geometry essentials shapes, angles, and spatial reasoning — from bamboo huts to bridge arches. the-magic-japi-hat basket-weavers-song bridge-that-grew types of angles what is an angle?

 an angle forms where two rays meet at a common point called the vertex. angles are measured in degrees (°).

 angle types by size:

 | type | range | example |
 |------|-------|---------|
 | acute | 0° < angle < 90° | tip of a bamboo leaf |
 | right | exactly 90° | corner of a book |
 | obtuse | 90° < angle < 180° | open laptop lid |
 | straight | exactly 180° | a flat line |
 | reflex | 180° < angle < 360° | more than halfway around |

 how to measure: place a protractor's centre on the vertex, align one ray with 0°, and read where the other ray crosses.

 key angle relationships:

 - complementary — two angles that add to 90° (e.g. 30° + 60°)
 - supplementary — two angles that add to 180° (e.g. 110° + 70°)
 - vertical angles — opposite angles where two lines cross. always equal

 these relationships are the building blocks of every geometric proof. angle relationships with parallel lines:

 when a transversal crosses two parallel lines, several angle pairs form:

 | relationship | rule |
 |-------------|------|
 | vertical angles | always equal |
 | alternate interior angles | equal |
 | co-interior (same-side) angles | sum to 180° |
 | corresponding angles | equal |

 worked example: two parallel lines cut by a transversal, one angle = 65°:

 | angle type | value | why |
 |-----------|-------|-----|
 | alternate interior | 65° | equal to given |
 | co-interior | 115° | 180° − 65° |
 | corresponding | 65° | equal to given |

 with just one known angle, you can find every angle in the figure. non-euclidean geometry changes the rules:

 - spherical (elliptic) — triangle angles sum to more than 180°. a triangle with vertices at the north pole, equator at 0°, and equator at 90°e has three 90° angles, totaling 270°
 - hyperbolic (saddle) — triangle angles sum to less than 180°
 - the angular excess (or deficit) is proportional to the triangle's area — the gauss-bonnet theorem links local geometry to global topology

 gps satellites must account for non-euclidean spacetime geometry (general relativity) to achieve metre-level accuracy. angleexplorerdiagram triangles and the pythagorean theorem the simplest polygon: a triangle has three sides, three angles, always summing to exactly 180°.

 classification by sides:

 | type | sides | angles |
 |------|-------|--------|
 | equilateral | all 3 equal | all 60° |
 | isosceles | 2 equal | 2 equal base angles |
 | scalene | none equal | all different |

 classification by angles: acute (all < 90°), right (one = 90°), obtuse (one > 90°).

 the pythagorean theorem

 for any right triangle with legs a and b and hypotenuse c:

 a² + b² = c²

 example: ladder against a wall

 | step | operation | result |
 |------|-----------|--------|
 | given | ladder = 5 m, base = 3 m | |
 | formula | height = sqrt(5² − 3²) | |
 | compute | sqrt(25 − 9) = sqrt(16) | 4 metres |

 the converse: if three sides satisfy a² + b² = c², the triangle must be a right triangle. the classic 3-4-5 triple (9 + 16 = 25) has been used by builders for thousands of years to create perfect right angles. pythagorean triples are integer solutions to a² + b² = c²:

 | triple | check |
 |--------|-------|
 | (3, 4, 5) | 9 + 16 = 25 |
 | (5, 12, 13) | 25 + 144 = 169 |
 | (8, 15, 17) | 64 + 225 = 289 |
 | (7, 24, 25) | 49 + 576 = 625 |

 generating primitive triples: a = m²−n², b = 2mn, c = m²+n² where m > n > 0, gcd(m,n) = 1, and m−n is odd.

 - m=2, n=1 gives (3, 4, 5)
 - m=3, n=2 gives (5, 12, 13)

 worked problem: a 13 m ladder, base 5 m from wall. height = sqrt(13² − 5²) = sqrt(169 − 25) = sqrt(144) = 12 metres. generalizations of the pythagorean theorem:

 - law of cosines: c² = a² + b² − 2ab·cos c works for any triangle. when c = 90°, cos 90° = 0 and it reduces to the pythagorean theorem
 - n-dimensional distance: d² = sum of (xi − yi)² — the n-dimensional pythagorean theorem
 - fermat's last theorem (proved by andrew wiles in 1995 after 358 years): a^n + b^n = c^n has no positive integer solutions for n > 2

 the proof used deep connections between elliptic curves and modular forms — mathematics far beyond anything fermat himself could have known. triangleexplorerdiagram what is a circle?

 a circle is the set of all points equidistant from a centre point. that distance is the radius (r).

 key measurements:

 | property | formula | notes |
 |----------|---------|-------|
 | diameter | d = 2r | longest line through centre |
 | circumference | c = 2 pi r | distance around |
 | area | a = pi r² | space enclosed |

 the number pi is approximately 3.14159 — the ratio of any circle's circumference to its diameter.

 circle vocabulary:

 - chord — a line segment connecting two points on the circle (diameter = longest chord)
 - tangent — a line touching the circle at exactly one point, always perpendicular to the radius at that point
 - arc — a portion of the circumference
 - sector — the "pie slice" between two radii and the arc they enclose

 sector area: (angle/360) x pi r²

 these properties appear everywhere — wheel design, gear ratios, clock faces, satellite orbits, and the circular cross-sections of bamboo used in traditional assamese homes. arc length and sector area (radians):

 - arc length: s = r x theta
 - sector area: a = (1/2) r² theta

 worked example: a pizza slice with r = 15 cm and angle = 45° (= pi/4 radians):

 | quantity | calculation | result |
 |----------|------------|--------|
 | arc length | 15 x pi/4 | 11.78 cm |
 | sector area | (1/2)(225)(pi/4) | 88.36 cm² |

 key theorems:

 - inscribed angle theorem — an inscribed angle is half the central angle subtending the same arc
 - thales' theorem — an angle inscribed in a semicircle is always 90° circle equation: (x−h)² + (y−k)² = r² for centre (h, k).

 in the complex plane, a circle of radius r centred at z0 is |z − z0| = r.

 - apollonius circles — loci where the distance ratio to two fixed points is constant — appear in electromagnetic field theory and optics
 - isoperimetric inequality — among all closed curves of a given perimeter, the circle encloses maximum area: a <= p²/(4 pi), with equality only for a circle
\

title: Git & Version Control
tagline: Track every change, collaborate without chaos, and never lose your work again.
searchText:
git & version control track every change, collaborate without chaos, and never lose your work again. what is version control? git basics: init, add, commit, status, log match each git command to what it does branches: creating, switching, and merging remote repositories: push, pull, clone, and github collaboration workflow: pull requests and code review directed acyclic graph (dag) working directory staging area repository commits should be atomic git flow trunk-based development interactive rebase cherry-pick bisect submodules git hooks git lfs forks pull requests code review conventional commits semantic versioning monorepos git-version-control git & version control track every change, collaborate without chaos, and never lose your work again. what is version control? imagine you are writing an essay and you save a new copy every time you make major changes: essayv1.docx, essayv2.docx, essayfinal.docx, essayfinalfinal.docx. within a week you have a dozen files and no idea which one has the paragraph you deleted yesterday. version control solves this by keeping a single file but recording every change as a snapshot in time. you can scroll back through history, see exactly what changed, who changed it, and why.

 this matters enormously when multiple people work on the same project. without version control, two developers editing the same file will overwrite each other's work. with it, the system tracks both sets of changes and helps merge them together. every professional software team in the world uses version control — it is as fundamental to coding as saving a document is to writing. git is the most popular version control system by far, created in 2005 by linus torvalds (who also created linux). git tracks changes, not files. each commit stores a snapshot plus metadata (author, timestamp, message, parent). the sha-1 hash uniquely identifies each commit. key commands: git init, git add file.py, git commit -m "message", git log --oneline, git diff, git status. the staging area lets you commit selectively: change 5 files, stage 2, commit those 2 — the rest remain uncommitted. the .gitignore file prevents tracking generated files (nodemodules/, pycache/, .env). git's data model is a directed acyclic graph (dag) of commits. branches are lightweight pointers — creating one is o(1). merges create two-parent commits. rebasing replays commits onto a new base for linear history. the reflog records every head movement, enabling recovery of "lost" commits. git's content-addressable storage deduplicates identical files across commits. pack files compress similar objects together, making repositories 10-50× smaller than storing full copies. the three-tree architecture (head, index, working directory) enables git's powerful staging workflow. git basics: init, add, commit, status, log git works in three stages. first, you make changes to files in your working directory — this is just your normal folder. second, you stage the changes you want to save using git add. staging is like placing items on a conveyor belt: you choose exactly which changes to include. third, you commit those staged changes with git commit -m "your message", which takes a permanent snapshot.

 the command git init creates a new repository (repo) in your folder — it tells git to start tracking changes here. git status shows you what has changed since the last commit: which files are modified, which are staged, and which are new. git log shows the history of all commits, newest first, with the author, date, and message for each. think of the log as a detailed diary of every save point in your project. these five commands — init, add, commit, status, and log — are the foundation of everything else in git. the three areas: working directory (your files), staging area (index — selected changes for next commit), repository (committed history). workflow: edit files → git add file.py (stage) → git commit -m "message" (save). git diff shows unstaged changes; git diff --staged shows staged changes. git log --oneline --graph shows branch history graphically. undo: git restore file.py discards working changes; git restore --staged file.py unstages. commits should be atomic — each commit represents one logical change with a clear message. git internals: a commit object contains a tree (snapshot of all files), parent pointer(s), author, and message. a tree object maps filenames to blob objects (file contents). blobs are content-addressed — identical file contents are stored once regardless of filename. git cat-file -p <hash> inspects any object. the .git directory contains: objects/ (blobs, trees, commits), refs/ (branch pointers), head (current branch pointer), index (staging area). understanding these internals helps debug unusual situations: detached head, lost commits, corrupted repositories. match each git command to what it does create a new repository and start tracking changes stage changes for the next commit git commit save a permanent snapshot of staged changes git status show which files have changed since the last commit display the history of all commits branches: creating, switching, and merging a branch is a parallel copy of your project where you can experiment without affecting the main version. imagine a tree: the trunk is your main code, and each branch grows in its own direction. you might create a branch called "add-dark-mode" to try a new feature. if it works out, you merge the branch back into the trunk. if it doesn't, you delete the branch and the main code is untouched.

 you create a branch with git branch feature-name and switch to it with git checkout feature-name (or the newer git switch feature-name). while on that branch, all your commits only affect that branch. when you are ready, you switch back to the main branch and run git merge feature-name to bring the changes in. the default branch is usually called main (older repos use master). branches are lightweight in git — they are just pointers to a commit, not full copies of files — so creating hundreds of branches costs almost nothing. professional teams create a new branch for every bug fix and every feature, keeping the main branch always stable and deployable. branch workflow: git branch feature-xyz (create), git switch feature-xyz (switch), make changes, commit, then git switch main; git merge feature-xyz. merge conflicts occur when both branches modify the same lines — manually edit, then git add and commit. git flow: main (production), develop (integration), feature/, release/, hotfix/. simpler: trunk-based development — short-lived branches merged frequently. github/gitlab pull requests enable code review before merging. advanced git: interactive rebase (git rebase -i head5) reorders, squashes, edits, or drops commits. cherry-pick (git cherry-pick abc123) applies a specific commit. bisect (git bisect start/bad/good) binary-searches through history to find bug-introducing commits in o(log n) steps. submodules manage repository dependencies. git hooks run scripts before/after events (pre-commit: lint code, pre-push: run tests). git lfs handles large binaries by storing pointers in the repo and actual files on a separate server. gitbranchdiagram remote repositories: push, pull, clone, and github so far, everything has been on your own computer. a remote repository is a copy of your repo stored on a server — most commonly on github, gitlab, or bitbucket. this serves two purposes: backup (your code survives even if your laptop dies) and collaboration (others can access the same repo).

 git clone url downloads a remote repo to your computer, including its full history. git push uploads your local commits to the remote. git pull downloads new commits from the remote and merges them into your local copy. think of push and pull like syncing a shared document: push sends your latest edits to the cloud, pull downloads everyone else's edits.

 github is the most popular hosting platform for git repositorie

title: Gravity & Orbits
searchText:
gravity & orbits orbits and satellites weightlessness escape velocity the rocket equation hohmann transfer orbits 686 n g(h) = gm/(r+h)² 8.67 m/s² u = −gmm/r −4.37 × 10⁹ j general relativity g_μν = hohmann transfer 3.93 km/s 5.25 hours restricted three-body problem orbital perturbations w_app = m(g − a) m × 11.81 n 14 m/s = 50 km/h equivalence principle geodesic riemann curvature tensor 1.3 × 10⁻⁵ m/s² spaghettification gradient f_tidal = 2gmmr/d³ 1.1 × 10⁻⁶ m/s² 5.1 × 10⁻⁷ m/s² 1.46× average 0.54× average tidal friction tidal locking roche limit tidal heating ½mv² − gmm/r = 0 v_esc = √(2gm/r) 11,186 m/s = 11.2 km/s 2,376 m/s = 2.4 km/s 0.414 × 7.9 = 3.3 km/s ke = ½mv² = gmm/r 6.25 × 10¹⁰ j ≈ 62.5 gj schwarzschild radius 2,953 m ≈ 3 km 8.87 mm tsiolkovsky rocket equation δv = v_e × ln(m₀ / m_f) mass ratio staging δv = v_e ln(m₀/m_f) = i_sp × g₀ × ln(m₀/m_f) worked example 4,740 m/s 9,316 m/s 14,056 m/s gravity loss drag loss thrust-to-weight ratio oberth effect nuclear thermal rockets fusion drives fuel-efficient hohmann transfer orbit ellipse circularize two short engine burns delta-v geostationary orbit 3.94 km/s 5.26 hours bi-elliptic transfer patched conic approximation gravity ' + 'assists gravity-and-orbits gravity & orbits kite-festival star-fell-deepor lotus-float newton's law of gravitation in 1687, isaac newton published his law of universal gravitation: every object with mass attracts every other object with mass. the force between two objects is given by f = gmm/r², where g is the gravitational constant (6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ n m²/kg²), m and m are the masses of the two objects, and r is the distance between their centres. this single equation explains everything from falling mangoes to orbiting planets. the force is always attractive, never repulsive, and it acts along the line connecting the two objects.

the inverse square law — the 1/r² in the equation — means that gravity weakens rapidly with distance. double the distance and the force drops to one-quarter. triple the distance and it falls to one-ninth. this is why the moon, despite being a quarter of earth's diameter, exerts only a tiny gravitational pull on you compared to the earth beneath your feet: the moon is 384,400 km away, while the ground is right under you. yet even at that distance, the moon's gravity is strong enough to raise the oceans in tides.

weight and mass are often confused but they are fundamentally different. mass is the amount of matter in an object, measured in kilograms — it does not change regardless of location. weight is the gravitational force acting on that mass: w = mg, where g is the local gravitational acceleration. on earth, g is approximately 9.81 m/s². on the moon, g is only 1.62 m/s², so a person with a mass of 60 kg would weigh 589 n on earth but only 97 n on the moon. their mass remains 60 kg in both places. astronauts on the international space station are often described as "weightless," but this is not because gravity has disappeared — it is because they are in continuous freefall, as we will discuss shortly. worked example: what is the gravitational force between the earth (m = 5.97 × 10²⁴ kg) and a 70 kg person standing on the surface (r = 6.371 × 10⁶ m)? f = gmm/r² = 6.674×10⁻¹¹ × 5.97×10²⁴ × 70 / (6.371×10⁶)² = 686 n (which is their weight — consistent with w = mg = 70 × 9.81). the gravitational acceleration at any altitude: g(h) = gm/(r+h)². at the iss (h = 408 km): g = 6.674×10⁻¹¹ × 5.97×10²⁴ / (6.779×10⁶)² = 8.67 m/s² (89% of surface gravity — astronauts are not beyond gravity's reach). gravitational potential energy: u = −gmm/r (negative because work must be done against gravity to separate objects). at the surface: u = −6.674×10⁻¹¹ × 5.97×10²⁴ × 70 / 6.371×10⁶ = −4.37 × 10⁹ j (this is the energy needed to remove a 70 kg person to infinity). newton's gravitation is the weak-field, low-velocity limit of einstein's general relativity (gr, 1915), which describes gravity not as a force but as the curvature of spacetime caused by mass-energy. the einstein field equations gμν = (8πg/c⁴)tμν relate spacetime curvature (gμν) to the distribution of mass-energy (tμν). for a spherically symmetric mass, the schwarzschild solution gives the metric and predicts: (1) gravitational time dilation — clocks tick slower in stronger gravity (confirmed by pound-rebka experiment, 1959, and gps corrections); (2) gravitational lensing — light bends around massive objects (first observed during the 1919 solar eclipse, now used to map dark matter); (3) gravitational redshift — photons lose energy climbing out of a gravity well; (4) black holes — when mass is compressed within the schwarzschild radius rs = 2gm/c² (for the sun: rs = 2.95 km; for earth: rs = 8.9 mm). the detection of gravitational waves by ligo (2015, nobel prize 2017) confirmed gr's prediction of propagating spacetime distortions travelling at exactly c. gravitationalfielddiagram orbits and satellites an orbit is the curved path an object follows when it moves fast enough to keep falling towards a body without ever hitting it. imagine firing a cannonball horizontally from a very tall mountain. fire it slowly and it hits the ground nearby. fire it faster and it travels further before landing. fire it at just the right speed and the curvature of its fall matches the curvature of the earth — it falls continuously but the ground curves away at the same rate. this is an orbit. in a circular orbit, gravity provides exactly the centripetal force needed to maintain the circular path: gmm/r² = mv²/r, which gives the orbital velocity v = sqrt(gm/r).

satellites orbit at different altitudes depending on their purpose. low earth orbit (leo) ranges from about 200 to 2,000 km altitude. the international space station orbits at 408 km, completing one orbit every 90 minutes at a speed of 27,600 km/h. at this altitude, it passes over different parts of the earth on each orbit, making it ideal for earth observation and scientific experiments. medium earth orbit (meo), at 2,000 to 35,786 km, is used by navigation satellites like gps, which orbit at about 20,200 km with a 12-hour period.

geostationary earth orbit (geo) is a special orbit at exactly 35,786 km above the equator, where the orbital period matches earth's rotation: 24 hours. a satellite in geo appears to hover motionless over one spot on the equator. this makes geo ideal for communications satellites and weather monitoring — your satellite tv dish can point at a fixed spot in the sky because the satellite never moves relative to the ground. india's insat series of weather and communication satellites operates from geo, providing continuous coverage of the indian subcontinent. orbital velocity for a circular orbit: v = √(gm/r). for leo at 400 km (r = 6,771 km = 6.771×10⁶ m): v = √(6.674×10⁻¹¹ × 5.97×10²⁴ / 6.771×10⁶) = 7,672 m/s = 27,620 km/h. orbital period: t = 2πr/v = 2π × 6.771×10⁶ / 7672 = 5,543 s = 92.4 minutes. for geo (t = 86,400 s): solving t = 2π√(r³/gm) gives r = (gmt²/4π²)^(1/3) = 42,164 km from earth's centre, or 35,786 km above the surface. kepler's third law: t² = (4π²/gm)r³. for any earth orbit: t² = 9.89 × 10⁻¹⁴ × r³ (with t in seconds, r in metres). gps satellites at r = 26,560 km: t = 2π√(2.656×10⁷)³/(6.674×10⁻¹¹ × 5.97×10²⁴)) = 43,077 s ≈ 11.97 hours. the total energy of an orbiting body: e = −gmm/(2r) — negative means bound. more energy (less negative) → higher orbit. real orbits are ellipses (kepler's first law), described by semi-major axis a and eccentricity e (0 = circle, 0-1 = ellipse, 1 = parabola, >1 = hyperbola). the vis-viva equation v² = gm(2/r − 1/a) gives the speed at any point in an elliptical orbit. at periapsis (closest approach): vp = √(gm(1+e)/(a(1−e))). to transfer between orbits, the hohmann transfer uses

title: Habitats & Biomes
tagline: From Kaziranga grasslands to Himalayan alpine meadows — where life thrives and why.
searchText:
habitats & biomes from kaziranga grasslands to himalayan alpine meadows — where life thrives and why. what defines a habitat match each habitat factor to its category terrestrial biomes aquatic ecosystems northeast india\ microhabitats habitat abiotic biotic species distribution models (sdms) maxent habitat fragmentation species–area relationship systematic conservation planning marxan zonation circuit theory biomes tropical rainforest tropical dry forest savanna/grassland desert temperate forest temperate grassland boreal forest/taiga tundra freshwater ecosystems marine ecosystems wetlands microhabitats habitats-and-biomes habitats & biomes from kaziranga grasslands to himalayan alpine meadows — where life thrives and why. kaziranga-grass snow-leopards-promise cloud-weaver-of-tawang dancing-deer-of-loktak-lake what defines a habitat match each habitat factor to its category temperature, rainfall, soil type abiotic (non-living) factors predators, food species, competitors biotic (living) factors kaziranga floodplain grasslands habitat for indian one-horned rhinoceros loktak lake floating phumdi mats habitat for the sangai deer — found nowhere else on earth a habitat is the natural environment where an organism lives, providing the food, water, shelter, and space it needs to survive and reproduce. every species has specific habitat requirements shaped by millions of years of evolution. the indian one-horned rhinoceros requires tall alluvial grasslands with nearby water sources for wallowing — exactly the conditions found in kaziranga's brahmaputra floodplain. the sangai deer of loktak lake needs floating phumdi mats, a habitat so specialized that it exists nowhere else on earth.

habitats are defined by both abiotic (non-living) and biotic (living) factors. abiotic factors include temperature, rainfall, soil type, sunlight, altitude, and water chemistry. biotic factors include the presence of food species, predators, competitors, parasites, and symbiotic partners. change any of these factors significantly and a habitat may become unsuitable for its current residents. when the ithai barrage raised water levels in loktak lake, it thinned the phumdi mats — the sangai's floating habitat literally sank, pushing the species to the edge of extinction.

habitat destruction is the single greatest threat to biodiversity worldwide, responsible for more species declines than pollution, hunting, or climate change. when a forest is cleared for agriculture, the habitat does not just shrink — it fragments into isolated patches too small to sustain viable populations, with roads, fields, and settlements creating barriers to movement between patches. habitat suitability is modelled using species distribution models (sdms) that correlate species occurrence records with environmental variables (temperature, rainfall, elevation, soil type) to predict where a species could live. maxent (maximum entropy modelling) is the most widely used algorithm, generating probability maps from presence-only data. for the indian rhinoceros, sdms identify the brahmaputra floodplain, terai grasslands, and parts of the duars as high-suitability habitat, matching observed populations. habitat fragmentation is measured by landscape metrics: patch size, edge-to-area ratio, nearest-neighbour distance, and connectivity indices. the species–area relationship s = ca^z (where s is species count, a is area, c and z are constants, z ≈ 0.15–0.35 for habitat islands) predicts that halving habitat area reduces species by 20–30%. conservation planning uses systematic conservation planning frameworks (margules & pressey, 2000) to identify priority areas. tools like marxan and zonation solve optimisation problems: which set of areas protects the most biodiversity at least cost? inputs include species occurrence, habitat maps, connectivity layers, land costs, and existing protected areas. for ne india, analyses consistently identify the kaziranga–karbi anglong corridor, the namdapha–kamlang complex, and meghalaya’s sacred groves as irreplaceable. circuit theory (mcrae et al., 2008) models animal movement across landscapes as electrical current flowing through a resistance surface, where low-resistance cells (forest) carry more current (animal movement) than high-resistance cells (roads, settlements). this approach has mapped optimal elephant corridors in assam, guiding highway design to include wildlife underpasses. terrestrial biomes climatezonesdiagram biomes are large-scale ecosystems classified by their dominant vegetation and climate. the major terrestrial biomes, arranged roughly from equator to poles, include: tropical rainforest (warm, wet year-round, with the highest biodiversity on earth), tropical dry forest (warm with distinct wet and dry seasons), savanna/grassland (warm with seasonal rainfall, dominated by grasses with scattered trees), desert (extremely low rainfall, below 250 mm per year), temperate forest (moderate temperatures with distinct seasons), temperate grassland (cold winters, hot summers, too dry for forests), boreal forest/taiga (long, cold winters, dominated by conifers like spruce and pine), and tundra (permafrost, extremely cold, treeless, with only mosses, lichens, and low shrubs).

each biome supports characteristic life forms adapted to its conditions. desert organisms conserve water through waxy coatings, nocturnal activity, and concentrated urine. tundra plants grow close to the ground to avoid wind and complete their life cycles in the brief summer. tropical rainforest trees grow towering canopies to compete for light, with more species of trees in a single hectare of amazonian forest (up to 300) than in all of northern europe.

altitude compresses these biome transitions into remarkably short distances. climbing from sea level to 5,000 metres in the himalayas, you pass through tropical, subtropical, temperate, subalpine, and alpine zones — the equivalent of travelling from equatorial india to the arctic — in just 100 km of horizontal distance. aquatic ecosystems watercyclediagram freshwater ecosystems — rivers, lakes, ponds, streams, and wetlands — cover less than 1% of earth's surface but support roughly 10% of all known animal species. rivers are dynamic systems where water flow, oxygen levels, temperature, and sediment load vary from headwaters to mouth. the brahmaputra's upper reaches in tibet are cold, fast-flowing, and oxygen-rich; by the time it reaches assam's floodplain, it is warm, slower, sediment-laden, and nutrient-rich, supporting a different community of organisms. lakes and wetlands (beels) in the brahmaputra valley are critical nurseries for fish, feeding grounds for migratory birds, and buffers against flooding.

marine ecosystems include the open ocean, coral reefs, estuaries, and coastal zones. coral reefs, sometimes called "rainforests of the sea," cover less than 0.1% of the ocean floor but support about 25% of marine fish species. estuaries — where rivers meet the sea — are among the most productive ecosystems on earth, mixing freshwater nutrients with saltwater to create nurseries for commercially important fish and shrimp. the sundarbans, where the ganges-brahmaputra delta meets the bay of bengal, is the world's largest mangrove forest.

wetlands deserve special attention because they are disproportionately valuable and disproportionately threatened. wetlands filter water, store carbon, buffer floods, recharge groundwater, and support extraordinary biodiversity. loktak lake in manipur, with its unique floating phumdis, is a ramsar wetland of international importance. yet globally, over 35% of wetlands have been lost since 1970 — drained for agriculture, filled for construction, or degraded by pollution. northeast india's biome diversity neindiabiomesdiagram northeast india is one of the most biologically remarkable regions on earth, containing representatives of nearly every major asian biome within a compact geographic area

title: Heat & Thermodynamics
searchText:
heat & thermodynamics entropy enthalpy carnot specific heat conduction convection radiation boltzmann temperature vs heat conduction, convection, radiation phase changes specific heat capacity laws of negative absolute temperatures third law q/t = ka(t_hot − t_cold)/l 6,000 w 65 w stefan-boltzmann law p = εσat⁴ 814 w navier-stokes equations rayleigh number nusselt number b(λ,t) = (2hc²/λ⁵) × 1/(exp(hc/λkt) − 1) kirchhoff's law of radiation q = ml 4,180 j 66,800 j 83,720 j 452,000 j 606,700 j ≈ 607 kj 71°c ehrenfest classification clausius-clapeyron equation supercritical fluids triple point 44°c 25.1°c c_v = (3/2)r = 12.47 j/(mol·k) c_v = (5/2)r = 20.79 j/(mol·k) equipartition theorem hydrogen bonding dulong-petit law δu = q − w 300 j 200 j η = 1 − t_cold/t_hot 65.3% δs = q/t 1,224 j/k clausius kelvin-planck s = k ln ω maxwell's demon 2.9 × 10⁻²¹ j radiative cooling blackbody 688 w 126 w wien's displacement law 501 nm 9,348 nm 10,063 nm b(λ,t) = (2hc²/λ⁵) / (exp(hc/(λkt)) − 1) rayleigh-jeans law wien's approximation passive radiative cooling evaporative cooling highest-energy sweating wet-bulb temperature matka latent heat of vaporization l_v = 2,260 kj/kg 2,450 kj/kg psychrometric equation dew point heat-and-thermodynamics heat & thermodynamics engineering specific heat conduction convection grandmothers-pitha holi-tea-gardens little-chef monsoon-home temperature vs heat temperature and heat are related but fundamentally different concepts, and confusing them is one of the most common mistakes in physics. temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of the particles (atoms or molecules) in a substance. when you heat water on a stove, the water molecules move faster — they vibrate, rotate, and translate more vigorously — and the temperature rises. the celsius scale sets 0°c at the freezing point of water and 100°c at its boiling point. the kelvin scale starts at absolute zero (−273.15°c), the theoretical temperature where all particle motion ceases. one kelvin degree is the same size as one celsius degree, so water freezes at 273.15 k and boils at 373.15 k.

heat, on the other hand, is the transfer of thermal energy between objects at different temperatures. it is measured in joules (j) or sometimes calories (1 calorie = 4.184 j). heat always flows spontaneously from a hotter object to a cooler one — from your freshly brewed assam tea into the cooler surrounding air, never the reverse. the amount of heat transferred depends on the temperature difference, the mass of the objects, and the material's specific heat capacity.

a crucial insight: a large object at a low temperature can contain more total thermal energy than a small object at a high temperature. a bathtub of warm water at 40°c contains far more thermal energy than a red-hot spark from a firecracker at 3,000°c, because the bathtub contains trillions of times more molecules. temperature tells you how energetic each molecule is on average; total thermal energy depends on both the temperature and the number of molecules. the relationship between heat transferred and temperature change is q = mcδt, where m is mass, c is specific heat capacity, and δt is the temperature change. worked example: heating 0.5 kg of water from 20°c to 100°c. q = 0.5 × 4186 × (100 − 20) = 167,440 j ≈ 167 kj. an electric kettle rated at 2000 w takes t = q/p = 167,440/2000 = 84 seconds. the kinetic theory links temperature to molecular motion: for an ideal gas, the average kinetic energy per molecule is ⟨ke⟩ = (3/2)kt, where k = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ j/k is boltzmann's constant. at room temperature (300 k): ⟨ke⟩ = 1.5 × 1.381×10⁻²³ × 300 = 6.21 × 10⁻²¹ j. the rms speed of a gas molecule is vrms = √(3kt/m). for n₂ at 300 k: vrms = √(3 × 1.381×10⁻²³ × 300 / 4.65×10⁻²⁶) = 517 m/s — faster than the speed of sound. temperature in statistical mechanics is defined via the boltzmann distribution: the probability of a system being in a state with energy e is p(e) ∝ exp(−e/kt). formally, 1/t = ∂s/∂e, where s is entropy and e is internal energy. this definition allows negative absolute temperatures — not "colder than zero" but "hotter than infinity." a population-inverted system (more atoms in the excited state than the ground state, as in a laser) has a negative temperature on this scale, because adding energy decreases entropy. such systems spontaneously transfer energy to any positive-temperature system. the third law implies that the heat capacity of all substances approaches zero as t → 0 k, which einstein (1907) and debye (1912) explained using quantum mechanics: at low t, the lattice vibration modes "freeze out" because kt is too small to excite them. the debye model predicts cv ∝ t³ at low temperatures, beautifully confirmed by experiment and one of the early triumphs of quantum theory. molecularmotiondiagram conduction, convection, radiation heat transfers between objects through three distinct mechanisms. conduction is heat transfer through direct molecular contact — fast-vibrating molecules bump into their slower neighbours, passing energy along. when you stir hot tea with a metal spoon, the handle warms up because the metal atoms at the submerged end vibrate rapidly and pass that energy up the handle atom by atom. metals are excellent conductors because their free electrons carry energy quickly. wood, cloth, and air are poor conductors (good insulators), which is why bamboo-handled cooking utensils stay cool in assamese kitchens and why the thatched roofs of traditional chang ghars keep interiors cool in summer and warm in winter.

convection is heat transfer through the bulk movement of a fluid (liquid or gas). when you heat water in a pot, the water at the bottom gets hot, expands, becomes less dense, and rises. cooler, denser water sinks to take its place, creating a circulation loop called a convection current. this same mechanism drives the monsoon: during summer, the indian subcontinent heats up faster than the indian ocean, creating rising air over land and drawing in moisture-laden ocean air — the southwest monsoon that brings 70-80% of assam's annual rainfall between june and september.

radiation is heat transfer through electromagnetic waves, requiring no medium at all. every object above absolute zero emits thermal radiation. the sun heats the earth through 150 million kilometres of vacuum entirely by radiation. darker surfaces absorb more radiation (which is why black roads get scorching hot in guwahati summers), while lighter surfaces reflect more (which is why traditional assamese houses are often whitewashed). at night, the ground radiates heat back into space, which is why clear winter nights in upper assam can bring frost even when daytime temperatures are mild — clouds act as a radiation blanket, trapping outgoing heat. the rate of heat conduction through a slab is q/t = ka(thot − tcold)/l, where k is thermal conductivity (w/(m·k)), a is area, and l is thickness. copper: k = 401 w/(m·k); glass: k = 1.0; wood: k = 0.15; air: k = 0.026. worked example: a single-pane glass window (k = 1.0, a = 1.5 m², l = 5 mm) with tin = 25°c, tout = 5°c. q/t = 1.0 × 1.5 × 20/0.005 = 6,000 w — enormous heat loss! double glazing (two 4 mm panes with 12 mm air gap): the air gap (k = 0.026) dominates resistance: q/t ≈ 0.026 × 1.5 × 20/0.012 = 65 w — a 100× reduction. the stefan-boltzmann law gives the power radiated by a hot body: p = εσat⁴, where σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ w/(m²·k⁴) and ε is emissivity (0-1). a human body (ε ≈ 0.98, a ≈ 1.7 m², t = 310 k) radiates p = 0.98 × 5.67×10⁻⁸ × 1.7 × 310⁴ = 814 w. convection is described by the navier-stokes equations coupled with the heat equation. the onset of natural convection is governed by the rayleigh number ra = gβδtl³/(να), where β is the thermal expansion coefficient, ν

tagline: The three languages every website is built with
searchText:
html / css / javascript the three languages every website is built with what the web actually is match each web technology to its role html — the structure css — the style javascript — the behavior how the three work together match the file type to what it contains the dom — your page as a tree responsive design — one page, every screen a complete html page styling with css interactive javascript putting it all together: a mini app building a search feature the dom — selecting & changing elements try dom manipulation events — responding to user actions try events forms — collecting user input flexbox — layout made simple try flexbox css grid — two-dimensional layout try css grid typescript + dom — type-safe web pages try typescript + dom types https document object model (dom) virtual dom dom (document object model) selecting elements: changing elements: creating elements: check yourself: event adding event listeners: common events: the event object `event.preventdefault()` basic form: input types: reading form values: `e.preventdefault()` validation: key properties on the container (parent): key properties on the children: css grid basic grid: named areas: when to use flexbox vs grid: flexbox grid the problem with plain javascript: typescript catches the issue: common dom types: typed event handlers: form data with typescript: html-css-js html / css / javascript the three languages every website is built with boy-who-built-a-library what the web actually is when you visit a website, your browser (chrome, firefox, safari) sends a request to a server somewhere in the world. the server sends back files — html, css, and javascript. your browser reads these files and renders the page you see. every website you have ever visited — google, youtube, wikipedia — is built from these same three languages. the browser is like a kitchen: html is the ingredients list, css is the plating instructions, and javascript is the cooking process that transforms everything into the final dish. the http request/response cycle: your browser sends get /index.html http/1.1 → the server responds with http/1.1 200 ok and the html content. status codes: 200=ok, 301=moved permanently, 404=not found, 500=server error. chrome devtools (f12) → network tab shows every request, its size, and load time. a typical web page makes 50-100 requests (html, css, js, images, fonts, analytics). https encrypts this traffic using tls — the padlock icon means a man-in-the-middle cannot read your data. http/2 multiplexes multiple requests over a single connection, and http/3 uses quic (over udp) for even faster connections. the document object model (dom) is a tree representation of the html that the browser constructs. javascript manipulates this tree: document.getelementbyid("title").textcontent = "new title" changes visible text without reloading the page. modern frameworks (react, vue, svelte) use a virtual dom — a lightweight javascript copy of the real dom — to compute the minimal set of changes needed, then batch-apply them. this diffing algorithm (o(n) heuristic instead of o(n³) exact tree comparison) is what makes single-page applications feel fast. web components (custom elements, shadow dom, templates) bring component-based architecture to vanilla html, reducing framework dependency. match each web technology to its role defines the structure and content of the page controls colors, fonts, spacing, and layout javascript makes the page interactive and dynamic reads html/css/js files and renders the page stores and sends website files when requested html — the structure htmltagsdiagram html (hypertext markup language) defines what is on the page. it uses tags — words in angle brackets — to label content.

says "this is a main heading."

says "this is a paragraph." places an image. creates a clickable link. tags usually come in pairs:

hello

— the opening tag, the content, and the closing tag. the browser reads these tags from top to bottom and lays out the page accordingly. html does not control colors, fonts, or animations — that is css\'s job. html only says "there is a heading here, a paragraph here, an image here." think of it as the blueprint of a house: it shows where the rooms and doors are, but not what color the walls are. semantic html elements convey meaning:

title: Human Body Systems
tagline: How your heart pumps, lungs breathe, and brain thinks — the engineering inside you.
searchText:
human body systems how your heart pumps, lungs breathe, and brain thinks — the engineering inside you. the circulatory system the respiratory system the digestive system the nervous system skeleton and muscles cardiac output (co) 5,040 ml/min ≈ 5 l/min 30.6 l/min co × total peripheral resistance (tpr) sinoatrial (sa) node atrioventricular (av) node bundle of his ecg frank-starling law intercalated discs lvad fick's law of diffusion rate = (d × a × δp) / t tidal volume vital capacity residual volume minute ventilation 7 l/min sigmoidal (s-shaped) dissociation curve hill coefficient n ≈ 2.8 bohr effect 2,3-bisphosphoglycerate (2,3-bpg) lock-and-key amylase pepsin trypsin lipase michaelis-menten kinetics v = v_max[s] / (k_m + [s]) gut-brain axis short-chain fatty acids faecal microbiota transplantation (fmt) action potential nodes of ranvier saltatory conduction 120 m/s snare proteins long-term potentiation (ltp) nmda receptors camkii ampa receptors optogenetics 65% inorganic 35% organic wolff's law sliding filament model motor units hill's equation (p + a)(v + b) = (p₀ + a)b type i type iix titin human-body-systems human body systems how your heart pumps, lungs breathe, and brain thinks — the engineering inside you. tigers-whisker honey-hunters-lesson red-panda-mask the circulatory system your heart is a muscular pump roughly the size of your fist, beating about 100,000 times a day to push blood through a network of blood vessels that, laid end to end, would stretch over 100,000 kilometres — enough to circle the earth two and a half times. the heart has four chambers: the two upper atria receive blood, and the two lower ventricles pump it out. the right side of the heart handles deoxygenated blood — blood that has delivered its oxygen to the body's tissues and picked up carbon dioxide waste. the right atrium receives this blood from the body via the superior and inferior vena cava, passes it to the right ventricle, which pumps it to the lungs through the pulmonary arteries. this is the pulmonary circuit.

in the lungs, blood releases carbon dioxide and picks up fresh oxygen (gas exchange), then returns to the left atrium via the pulmonary veins. the left ventricle — the most muscular chamber — pumps this oxygen-rich blood into the aorta and out to every tissue in the body through the systemic circuit. arteries carry blood away from the heart (remember: a for away), branching into smaller arterioles and then into capillaries — vessels so narrow that red blood cells must pass through in single file. it is in the capillaries that the actual exchange occurs: oxygen and nutrients diffuse out into surrounding tissues, while carbon dioxide and waste products diffuse in. the blood then collects into venules, then veins, and returns to the right atrium to begin the cycle again.

your pulse is the rhythmic expansion of an artery as the left ventricle contracts and sends a wave of blood through the arterial system. a resting heart rate of 60–100 beats per minute is normal for most people. during exercise, the heart rate increases to deliver more oxygen to working muscles — a trained athlete's heart can pump up to 30 litres of blood per minute compared to about 5 litres at rest. blood pressure, measured in millimetres of mercury (mmhg), has two numbers: systolic (pressure during a heartbeat) over diastolic (pressure between beats). a healthy reading is around 120/80 mmhg. valves between chambers and in veins ensure blood flows in one direction only — the "lub-dub" sound of your heartbeat is the sound of these valves snapping shut. cardiac output (co) = heart rate × stroke volume. at rest: co = 72 bpm × 70 ml = 5,040 ml/min ≈ 5 l/min. during intense exercise: co = 180 bpm × 170 ml = 30.6 l/min (6× increase). blood pressure is governed by co × total peripheral resistance (tpr): bp = co × tpr. the left ventricle wall is 3× thicker than the right because it must generate 120 mmhg (systemic) vs 25 mmhg (pulmonary). the heart's electrical system controls the rhythm: the sinoatrial (sa) node (natural pacemaker) fires at 72/min → signal spreads through atria → atrioventricular (av) node delays 0.1 s (allowing atria to empty) → bundle of his → purkinje fibres → ventricles contract. an ecg (electrocardiogram) records this: the p wave = atrial depolarisation, qrs complex = ventricular depolarisation, t wave = ventricular repolarisation. the frank-starling law states that stroke volume increases with end-diastolic volume (preload): more blood filling the ventricle stretches cardiac myocytes, optimising actin-myosin overlap and increasing contraction force. this intrinsic mechanism matches output to venous return without neural input. at the cellular level, cardiac muscle has unique features: intercalated discs with gap junctions allow rapid electrical coupling (the heart functions as a syncytium), and t-tubules positioned at z-lines (vs. a-i junctions in skeletal muscle) ensure synchronous calcium release. calcium-induced calcium release (cicr) from the sarcoplasmic reticulum amplifies the l-type ca²⁺ current. heart failure involves impaired serca2a (sr calcium pump) and elevated phospholamban inhibition, reducing diastolic calcium reuptake. modern treatments include lvad (left ventricular assist devices) — continuous-flow pumps that augment output in end-stage heart failure, with >80% two-year survival rates. heartdiagram the respiratory system the respiratory system's job is to bring oxygen into the body and expel carbon dioxide — the gaseous waste product of cellular respiration. air enters through the nose or mouth, where it is warmed, moistened, and filtered by mucus and tiny hairs (cilia) that trap dust and pathogens. it then passes through the pharynx (throat) and larynx (voice box) into the trachea (windpipe), a tube reinforced by c-shaped rings of cartilage that prevent it from collapsing. the trachea branches into two bronchi — one for each lung — which subdivide repeatedly into smaller bronchioles, like the branches of a tree becoming finer and finer twigs.

at the tips of the smallest bronchioles are clusters of tiny air sacs called alveoli — about 300 million of them in each lung, giving a total surface area of roughly 70 square metres (about the size of a tennis court). each alveolus is surrounded by a dense mesh of capillaries. the walls of both the alveolus and the capillary are just one cell thick, so oxygen and carbon dioxide need to diffuse across a distance of only about 0.5 micrometres. oxygen dissolves in the thin film of moisture lining the alveolus, diffuses across the membrane into the blood, and binds to haemoglobin in red blood cells. simultaneously, carbon dioxide diffuses from the blood into the alveolus to be exhaled. this exchange is driven entirely by concentration gradients — gases naturally move from areas of high concentration to low concentration.

breathing is powered by the diaphragm, a dome-shaped muscle beneath the lungs. when you inhale, the diaphragm contracts and flattens, and the intercostal muscles between your ribs contract to lift the rib cage outward. this increases the volume of the thoracic cavity, reducing air pressure inside the lungs below atmospheric pressure, so air rushes in. when you exhale, the diaphragm relaxes back into its dome shape, the rib cage drops, thoracic volume decreases, pressure inside the lungs rises above atmospheric pressure, and air is pushed out. at rest, you breathe about 12–20 times per minute, moving roughly half a litre of air with each breath. during vigorous exercise, both the rate and depth of breathing increase dramatically to meet the muscles' heightened demand for oxygen. gas exchange at the alveoli follows fick's law of diffusion: rate = (d × a × δp) / t, where d is the diffusion coefficient, a is surface area (70 m²), δp is the partial pressure difference, and t is membrane thickness (0.5 μm). for o₂: alveolar po₂ ≈ 104 mmhg, deoxygenated blood po₂ ≈ 40 mmhg, so δp = 64
